content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 4, 2018

Ben Hafer
1521 Mayfield Street
Sacramento, CA 95835

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-18-202

Dear Mr. Hafer:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your inquiry is general in nature we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

Please note that we are only providing advice under the post-governmental employment provisions of the Act. Therefore, we offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other post-governmental employment laws that may apply, such as Public Contract Code Section 10411, or Section 1090.

Also note, we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

As a former state employee with the Office of Systems Integration ("OSI") are you precluded under the Act from acting as a private consultant on the Department of Health Care Services' ("CDHCS") Medical Management Information Replacement Project ("MMIR Project"); or generally with the California Health and Human Services Agency ("CHHS"), with the OSI, or with the California Department of Technology ("CDT")?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-18-202
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

You are subject to the one-year ban to the extent that your consultant work involves
communications or appearances before OSI, your former agency, as detailed below. Your facts
indicate that you did not work for, or represent, CDHCS or CDT in the past, and neither department
had control over OSI operations. Therefore, the one-year ban does not extend to those departments.
Nor does it extend to CHHS, because you did not work for, or represent, CHHS and the facts
indicate that OSI had a separate budget, personnel and operations from CHHS.

You are subject to the permanent ban as well, in regards to any proceedings you participated
in as a Technical Manager at OSI. You have identified one specific proceeding, the CWDS project,
in which you previously participated. The permanent ban will apply to this project.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY THE REQUESTOR

You left state service on July 6, 2018 after working at the Office of Systems Integration
("OSI") for 10 years as a Technical Manager. OSI was established within the CHHS Agency³ to
manage a portfolio of large, complex health and human services information technology ("IT")
projects. In this capacity, OSI coordinates communication, collaboration and decision-making
among project stakeholders and program-side sponsors of the projects. OSI manages the
procurement, contract negotiations and contract management aspects of the acquisition of
technology systems and services. After the procurement phase, OSI oversees the design,
development, governance and implementation of IT systems which serve health and human services
programs.⁴ In our conversations by phone and in emails, you indicated your understanding that OSI
controls its own budget, its personnel management is independent of the CHHS, and it has
independent operational controls including its own human resources, administration, and
information technology departments.

At OSI you specifically worked on the Child Welfare Digital Services ("CWDS") project
and its different iterations.⁵ CWDS is a collaboration of state and local government agencies
dedicated to building a new child welfare information system that responds to users' needs while
maintaining standards for security and data integrity. In the role of Technical Manager, you wrote
most of the technical contracts used by the CWDS project. You worked with the CDT procurement
team, which provided oversight and approval authority, once these contracts went out for the
solicitation of bids. You participated in evaluating and selecting winning bidders. You describe
your interaction with CDT as a customer engaged in CDT's required procurement oversight role.
Your supervisor had signatory authority on the actual contracts, and you managed OSI staff on the
performance of the contracts. You state that the majority of these contracts are in a completion
phase or have been completed. Further, the contracts that you were involved in while working at

³ The California Health and Human Services Agency oversees sixteen state departments or offices, including
CDHCS, and OSI.

⁴ See http://www.osi.ca.gov/AboutUs.shtml for background information on OSI, website as of November 5,
2018.

⁵ These include the Child Welfare Services / Web Project ("CWS/Web") and the Child Welfare Services –
New System Project ("CWS-NS").

File No. 1-18-202
Page No. 3

OSI are not at issue in your request. You filed a Statement of Economic Interest, Form 700 in your
position.

You are interested in performing consulting services as an independent contractor or as an
employee of a consulting firm doing business for the State. You understand that you would not be
able to work in any relation to any contracts you managed, or were responsible for, related to
CWDS. Your inquiry is whether you are precluded from working on CDT matters, on other OSI
projects, or projects within other departments of CHHS such as CDHCS. You did not work for or
represent CHHS during your employment at OSI. Nor have you worked on CDHCS' MMIR
Project.

ANALYSIS

Under the Act, public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-
governmental employment provisions known as the one-year ban and the permanent ban. (Sections
87400-87406 and Regulation 18746.1.)

One-Year Ban

The one-year ban prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any
formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her
former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions, or any
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, award, or revocation of a permit, license,
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406; Regulation
18746.1.)

The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency "who holds a
position that entails the making, or participation in the making, of decisions that may foreseeably
have a material effect on any financial interest." This may be evidenced by the employee holding a
position that is designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code or by the actual duties of the
employee. (Section 87406(d)(1); Regulation 18746.1(a)(4).) The ban applies for twelve months
from the date the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. The one-year ban
applies to you based on your duties at OSI and the fact that you filed a Form 700 each year.

The one-year ban applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for
his or her appearances or communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person
as an agent, attorney, or representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3) and (4).) In
contrast to the permanent ban (discussed below), which only applies to certain "judicial or quasi-
judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban applies if the appearance or communication is made for the
purpose of influencing any of the following actions:

File No. 1-18-202
Page No. 4

. . . administrative or legislative action, or influencing any action or proceeding
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property.

(Section 87406(d)(1).)⁶

An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the
"principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2(a).) An appearance or communication
includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Ibid.)

Furthermore, Regulation 18746.1(b)(6)(C)(i) and (ii) states that appearances and
communications are prohibited, as relevant to your facts, when they are (1) before a state agency
that the public official worked for or represented; or (2) before a state agency whose budget,
personnel, and other operations are subject to the control of a state agency the public official
worked for or represented.⁷

The one-year ban applies to you. During this period, you may not appear before or
communicate with OSI for compensation, for the purpose of influencing administrative or
legislative actions or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or
property. You may, however, provide policy review and advice to a third-party contractor, if you
are not identified with these efforts and so long as you are not otherwise prohibited from such
representation under the permanent ban. Additionally, there is an exception which allows the
performance of services to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit,
license, grant, contract, or sale agreement to the extent that such services do not involve changes to
any of these actions, and so long as the services are not prohibited under the permanent ban.
(Regulation 18746.1(c).)

Your facts indicate that you did not work for, or represent, CDHCS or CDT in the past, and
neither department had control over the OSI operations. Therefore, the one-year ban does not
extend to those departments. As to CHHS, you did not work for or represent CHHS, and OSI had a
separate budget, personnel and operations from CHHS. The one-year ban does not extend to this
agency.

Permanent Ban

The permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from switching sides and participating
for compensation in certain proceedings involving the State of California and other specific parties,

⁶ See Section 82002(a) and Section 82037, respectively, for a detailed definition of "administrative" and
"legislative actions."

⁷ Regulation 18746.1(b)(6)C)(ii) provides that the provision of technical assistance or legal advice, or
oversight, by another agency under state law are not factors to consider to determine whether an agency is subject to the
direction and control of another agency.

File No. I-18-202
Page No. 5

if the former state employee participated in the proceeding(s) while employed by the state.
(Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.)

The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any
oral or written communication made with the intent to influence a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which you participated while serving as a state administrative official. (Section
87401.) These types of proceedings include: any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or
other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other
particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency.
(Section 87400(e).) Thus, the permanent ban covers proceedings that affect the rights or claims of
specific parties. It does not apply to proceedings that involve the making of rules or policies of
general applicability.

An official or employee has "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the
proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential
information ...." (Section 87400(d).) As a former manager, you are deemed to have participated in
any proceeding that was "pending before"8 your agency and that was under your supervisory
authority. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(4).) A proceeding was under your "supervisory authority" if any
of the following apply:

(A) The supervisor's duties include the primary responsibility within the
agency for directing the operation or function of the program where the
proceeding is initiated or conducted. However, this provision does not apply
to a supervisor who is only responsible for the general oversight of the
administrative actions or functions of a program in which the responsibilities
concerning the specific or final review of the proceeding are expressly
delegated to other persons in the agency.

(B) The supervisor directly supervises the person performing the
investigation, review, or other action involved in the proceeding including,
but not limited to, assigning the matter for which the required conduct is
taken.

(C) The supervisor reviews, discusses, or authorizes any action in the
proceeding.

(D) The supervisor has contact with any of the participants in the proceeding
regarding the subject of the proceeding.

(Regulation 18741.1(a)(4).)

You are subject to the permanent ban as a former Technical Manager at OSI. To apply the
permanent ban to your situation, you must identify the proceedings in which you participated while
employed by the state. The permanent ban does not apply to projects in which you did not

8 This term is defined in Regulation 18438.2.

File No. I-18-202
Page No. 6

participate. You have identified one specific proceeding, the CWDS project, in which you
previously participated as a Technical Manager at OSI. The permanent ban will apply to this
project.

While you have stated that you do not intend to work on any proceedings related to the
CWDS project, we note that the permanent ban does not apply to "new" proceedings even in cases
where the new proceeding is related to, or grows out of, a prior proceeding in which the official had
participated. New contracts with the employee's former agency in which the employee did
not participate are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A new
proceeding typically involves different parties, a different subject matter, or different factual issues
from those considered in previous proceedings. (Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-119.)

Further, the application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is
considered a proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or
approval. (See Staroveytov Advice Letter, No. A-14-149, citing Anderson Advice Letter No. A-98-
159, and Blonien Advice Letter, No. A-89-463.) The "performance" or "implementation"
proceeding is narrowly construed and limited to the execution of the existing terms of an existing
contract. (Lujan Advice Letter, No. A-14-009.) Where an employee has participated in the
implementation proceeding, the employee may not switch sides and work for compensation for an
outside employer on the same implementation proceeding. (Culp Advice Letter, No. I-14-051.)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

sections.analysis:
Under the Act, public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-
governmental employment provisions known as the one-year ban and the permanent ban. (Sections 
87400--87406 and Regulation 18746.1.) 
One-Year Ban 
The one-year ban prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any 
formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her 
former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions, or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, award, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406; Regulation 
18746.1.) 
< 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency "who holds a 
position that entails the making, or participation in the making, of decisions that may foreseeably 
have a material effect on any financial interest." This may be evidenced by the employee holding a 
position that is designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code or by the actual duties of the 
employee. (Section 87406(d)(l); Regulation 18746.l(a)(4):) The ban applies for twelve months 
from the date the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. The one-year ban 
applies to you based on your duties at OSI and the fact that you filed a Form 700 each year. 
The one-year ban applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for 
his or her appearances or communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person 
as an agent, attorney, or representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(3) and (4).) In 
contrast to the permanent ban (discussed below), which only applies to certain "judicial or quasi-
judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban applies if the appearance or communication is made for the 
purpose of influencing any of the following actions:  
... administrative or legislative action, or influencing any action or proceeding 
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or p~rchase of goods or property. 
(Section 87406(d)(l).) 6 
An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the 
"principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or 
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2(a).) An appearance or communication 
includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by 
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Ibid.) 
Furthermore, Regulation 18746.l(b)(6)(C)(i) and (ii) states that appearances and 
communications are prohibited, as relevant to your facts, when they are (1) before a state agency 
that the public official worked for or represented; or (2) before a state agency whose budget, 
personnel, and other operations are subject to the control of a state agency the public official 
worked for or represented. 7 
The one-year ban applies to you. During this period, you may not appear before or 
communicate with OSI for compensation, for the purpose of influencing administrative or 
legislative actions or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, 
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or 
property. You may, however, provide policy review and advice to a third-party contractor, if you 
are not identified with these efforts and su long as you are not otherwise prohibited from such 
representation under the permanent ban. Additionally, there is an exception which allows the 
performance of services to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, 
license, grant, contract, or sale agFeement to the extent that such services do not involve changes to 
any of these actions, and so long as the services are not prohibited under the permanent ban. 
(Regulation 18746.l(c).) 
Your facts indicate that you did not work for, or represent, CDHCS or CDT in the past, and 
neither department had control over the OSI operations. Therefore, the one-year ban does not 
extend to those departments. As to CHHS, you did not work for or represent CHHS, and OSI had a 
separate budget, personnel and operations from CHHS. The one-year ban does not extend to this 
agency. 
Permanent Ban 
The permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from switching sides and participating 
for compensation in certain proceedings involving the State of California and other specific parties, 
6 See Section 82002(a) and Section 82037, respectively, for a detailed definition of 'administrative" and 
"legislative actions." 
· 
7 Regulation 187 46.1 (b )( 6)(C)(ii) provides that the provision of technical assistance or legal advice, or 
oversight, by another agency under state law are not factors to consider to determine whether an agency is subject to the 
direction and control of another agency.  
if the former state employee participated in the proceeding(s) while employed by the state. 
(Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.) 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or lnformal appearance or any 
oral or written communication made with the intent to influence a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other 
proceeding in which you participated while serving as a state administrative official. (Section 
87401.) These types of proceedings include: any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or 
other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other 
particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency. 
(Section 87400(c).) Thus, the permanent ban covers proceedings that affect the rights or claims of 
specific parties. It does not apply to proceedings that involve the making of rules or policies of 
general applicability. 
An official or employee has "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the 
proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written 
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential 
information .... " (Section 87400(d).) As a former manager, you are deemed to have participated in 
any proceeding that was "pending before"8 your agency and that was under your supervisory 
authority. (Regulation 18741. l(a)(4).) A proceeding was under your "supervisory authority" if any 
of the following apply: 
· 
(A) The supervisor's duties include the primary responsibility within the 
agency for directing the operation or function of the program where the 
proceeding is initiated or conducted. However, this provision does not apply 
to a supervisor who is only responsible for the general oversight of the 
administrative actions or functions of a program in which the responsibilities 
concerning the specific or final review of the proceeding are expressly · 
delegated to other persons in the agency. 
(B) The supervisor directly supervises the person performing the 
investigation, review, or other action involved in the proceeding including, 
but not limited to, assigning the matter for which the required conduct is 
taken. 
(C) The supervisor reviews, discusses, or authorizes any action in the 
proceeding. 
(D) The supervisor has contact with any of the participants in the proceeding 
regarding the subject of the proceeding. 
(Regulation 18741.1 (a)( 4 ).) 
You are subject to the permanent ban as a former Technical Manager at OSI. To apply the 
permanent ban to your situation, you must identify the proceedings in which you participated while 
employed by the state. The permanent ban does not apply to projects in which you did not 
8 This term is defined in Regulation 18438.2.  
participate. You have identified one specific proceeding, the CWDS project, in which you 
previously participated as a Technical Manager at OSI. The permanent ban will apply to this 
project. 
While you have stated that you do not intend to work on any proceedings related to the 
CWDS project, we note that the permanent ban does not apply to "new" proceedings even in cases 
where the new proceeding is related to, or grows out of, a prior proceeding in which the official had 
participated. New contracts with the employee's former agency in which the former employee did 
not participate are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A new 
proceeding typically involves different parties, a different subject matter, or different factual issues 
from those considered in previous proceedings. (Donovan Advice Letter, No. 1-03-119.) 
Further, the application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is 
considered a proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or 
approval. (See Starovoytov Advice Letter, No. A-14-149, citing Anderson Advice Letter No. A-98-
159, and Blonien Advice Letter, No. A-89-463.) The "performance" or "implementation" 
proceeding is narrowly construed and limited to the execution of the existing terms of an existing 
contract. (Lujan Advice Letter, No, A-14-009.) Where an employee has participated in the 
implementation proceeding, the employee may not switch sides and work for compensation for an 
outside employer on the same implementation proceeding. (Culp Advice Letter, No. I-14-051.)

represent CHHS during your employment at OSI. Nor have you worked on CDHCS' MMIR 
Project.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 December 4, 2018 Ben Hafer 1521 Mayfield Street Sacramento, CA 95835 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-18-202 Dear Mr. Hafer: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your inquiry is general in nature we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Please note that we are only providing advice under the post-governmental employment provisions of the Act. Therefore, we offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other post-governmental employment laws that may apply, such as Public Contract Code Section 10411, or Section 1090. Also note, we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTION As a former state employee with the Office of Systems Integration ("OSI") are you precluded under the Act from acting as a private consultant on the Department of Health Care Services' ("CDHCS") Medical Management Information Replacement Project ("MMIR Project"); or generally with the California Health and Human Services Agency ("CHHS"), with the OSI, or with the California Department of Technology ("CDT")? ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) File No. I-18-202 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION You are subject to the one-year ban to the extent that your consultant work involves communications or appearances before OSI, your former agency, as detailed below. Your facts indicate that you did not work for, or represent, CDHCS or CDT in the past, and neither department had control over OSI operations. Therefore, the one-year ban does not extend to those departments. Nor does it extend to CHHS, because you did not work for, or represent, CHHS and the facts indicate that OSI had a separate budget, personnel and operations from CHHS. You are subject to the permanent ban as well, in regards to any proceedings you participated in as a Technical Manager at OSI. You have identified one specific proceeding, the CWDS project, in which you previously participated. The permanent ban will apply to this project. FACTS AS PRESENTED BY THE REQUESTOR You left state service on July 6, 2018 after working at the Office of Systems Integration ("OSI") for
